text:
COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
END-OF-TREATMENT RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Marguerite Okonkwo is a 47-year-old woman with a known history of FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary (bilateral primary, pT3cN1M0), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), status post primary debulking surgery (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, radical omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, appendectomy, right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping, and sigmoid mesenteric implant resections) with complete gross resection (R0) achieved. Patient has subsequently completed 6 cycles of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 6) and paclitaxel (175 mg/m2) administered every 21 days, with cycles 5 and 6 delivered via 12-step carboplatin desensitization protocol following a grade 2 carboplatin hypersensitivity reaction during cycle 4. Serum CA-125 at end of treatment: 11 U/mL (normalized). This examination is performed for end-of-chemotherapy restaging to assess treatment response and establish a new radiographic baseline prior to initiation of maintenance PARP inhibitor therapy.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed after cycle 3 of chemotherapy (interim restaging), as well as the pre-operative staging CT obtained at the time of initial diagnosis. Additionally compared to CT obtained at initial diagnosis demonstrating the index bilateral ovarian masses, omental caking, peritoneal carcinomatosis, pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy, and large-volume ascites.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast material via power injector through an implanted port-a-cath, with imaging obtained in the portal venous phase. Standard axial reconstructions were generated at 3 mm intervals. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats were generated and reviewed. Lung window settings used for evaluation of pulmonary parenchyma.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidations are identified. There is no ground-glass opacity or interstitial thickening to suggest lymphangitic carcinomatosis. The airways are patent to the level of the segmental bronchi bilaterally. Previously, at the time of initial staging, the lower lung bases on the abdominopelvic CT were unremarkable; the dedicated chest CT likewise showed no pulmonary involvement. No new pulmonary abnormalities are demonstrated on the current examination.

Pleural Spaces: No pleural effusion is identified bilaterally. There is no pleural thickening or pleural nodularity. Notably, large-volume ascites was present at initial diagnosis; no sympathetic or malignant pleural effusion is present at this time.

Mediastinum and Hila: The heart is normal in size. There is no pericardial effusion. The mediastinum is midline. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal masses are seen. The aorta and main pulmonary artery are normal in caliber. No mediastinal or hilar lymph node enlargement is present. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the axillary, supraclavicular, or internal mammary chains. The thyroid gland, insofar as visualized on this study, appears unremarkable.

Chest Wall and Bony Thorax: No focal osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic disease. No rib lesions or sternal abnormalities. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine are noted, without aggressive features.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously without focal lesion. No hepatic masses, hypodense nodules, or enhancing foci are identified to suggest parenchymal metastatic disease. The biliary system is not dilated. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions are identified. No perisplenic or splenic surface nodularity.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously throughout its head, body, and tail. No pancreatic mass or ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic soft tissue stranding.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal with no nodular enlargement or thickening.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys show symmetric cortical enhancement without focal parenchymal lesions. No hydronephrosis. No perinephric soft tissue nodularity or infiltration. The ureters are not dilated.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The stomach and visualized small bowel loops are normal in caliber without obstruction or wall thickening. Post-appendectomy status is noted, with expected absence of the appendix and minor post-operative soft tissue changes in the right lower quadrant. The colon and rectosigmoid demonstrate normal wall thickness without pericolic soft tissue implants or pericolonic nodularity.

Omentum and Peritoneum: This is a critical area of assessment given the prior omental caking and peritoneal carcinomatosis at initial diagnosis. The surgical bed of the resected omentum demonstrates expected post-operative fatty and fibrous changes without evidence of residual or recurrent omental infiltration. There is no new soft tissue thickening or nodularity within the greater omentum footprint. No peritoneal implants are identified along the right or left paracolic gutters, the surface of the liver or spleen, the right hemidiaphragm peritoneum (site of prior peritoneal stripping with surgical clips visible), the sigmoid mesentery, or the pelvic peritoneum. No miliary nodularity. The peritoneal surfaces appear smooth and without abnormal enhancement.

Ascites: No free intraperitoneal fluid is detected. The abdomen and pelvis are dry. This represents a marked and complete resolution from the large-volume ascites (clinically estimated at 4.5 liters) present at initial diagnosis.

Retroperitoneum and Mesentery: The retroperitoneum is unremarkable. No enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes are identified. In particular, the para-aortic region at the L2 level, which previously harbored an enlarged lymph node cluster measuring 1.8 cm in aggregate (involved by metastatic carcinoma at surgical pathology), now shows no lymphadenopathy. The retroperitoneal fat planes are preserved without soft tissue infiltration.

---

PELVIS:

Post-Surgical Anatomy: Expected post-operative changes are identified following total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The uterus and bilateral adnexal structures are surgically absent. The vaginal cuff is intact and unremarkable in appearance. Surgical clips are present in the expected locations of the bilateral pelvic sidewalls and para-aortic region, consistent with the prior lymph node dissection beds. Pelvic floor soft tissues are unremarkable.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: The bilateral pelvic nodal chains, including the right and left external iliac, internal iliac, obturator, and common iliac stations, are normal in size. The previously identified enlarged right external iliac lymph node (2.1 cm at diagnosis, confirmed positive for carcinoma on surgical pathology) is no longer present. No pathologically enlarged pelvic lymph nodes are identified.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is normally distended with smooth wall contours and no intraluminal filling defects. No perivesical soft tissue stranding.

Rectosigmoid and Pelvic Bowel: The rectosigmoid colon demonstrates a normal wall thickness without masses or pericolonic nodularity. The site of prior sigmoid mesenteric implant resections demonstrates post-operative fibrous changes without evidence of recurrent soft tissue nodularity.

Bony Pelvis: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified within the imaged bony pelvis. Mild degenerative changes at the sacroiliac joints, without aggressive features.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. COMPLETE RADIOGRAPHIC RESPONSE to adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy following primary debulking surgery. No radiographically identifiable residual or recurrent malignancy is demonstrated on this examination.

2. Total resolution of the previously described large-volume ascites. The peritoneal surfaces are smooth and clear with no identifiable peritoneal implants, no omental nodularity, and no soft tissue deposits at any of the sites of prior peritoneal carcinomatosis.

3. Complete resolution of the previously enlarged pelvic and para-aortic lymph nodes. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the pelvic, retroperitoneal, mediastinal, or hilar regions.

4. No evidence of hepatic parenchymal metastases, pulmonary metastases, or pleural disease.

5. Post-operative findings are consistent with prior primary debulking surgery including total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, radical omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping, and sigmoid mesenteric implant resections. All expected post-surgical changes are present and unremarkable.

6. Niraparib maintenance therapy baseline established: this examination serves as the new radiographic reference standard prior to initiating PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy.

Overall, the findings are consistent with a robust and complete response to systemic chemotherapy. No imaging evidence of active malignant disease is present at this time.
